extra.message: User-controlled input `search_query` from `request.args.get('search', '')` is directly interpolated into an SQL query string using an f-string: `f"SELECT filename FROM files WHERE user_id = {user_id} AND filename LIKE '%{search_query}%'"`. This allows an attacker to inject arbitrary SQL via the `search` query parameter.

cwe: CWE-89: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection')

extra.message: The `user.country` field is rendered with Jinja2's `| safe` filter, which disables auto-escaping. If an attacker stores a malicious HTML/JavaScript payload in their country field (e.g., via the signup or update_profile forms), it will be executed in the victim's browser as stored XSS.

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

extra.message: The `search_query` value from `request.args.get('search')` is reflected into the HTML input value attribute using `{{ search_query | safe }}`, bypassing Jinja2's auto-escaping. An attacker can craft a URL with a malicious `search` parameter to inject HTML/JavaScript into the page, resulting in reflected XSS.

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

extra.message:
Unsanitized user input from a comment text field is appended directly to the DOM via jQuery's `append()` with HTML string concatenation: `$commentList.append('
  • ' + comment + '
    • ')`. This allows DOM-based XSS: any user who types `` in the comment box will have it executed immediately in their own browser, and if comments were ever persisted server-side this would become stored XSS.

cwe: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

extra.message: The `DELETE /api/v1/delete-user/<user_id>` endpoint has authentication (`@token_required`) commented out (lines 221-222). Any unauthenticated request can delete any user by simply supplying their numeric `user_id` in the URL. There is no session check, no token verification, and no authorization check to ensure the caller owns or is an admin of the target account.

cwe: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

extra.message: The `GET /api/v1/profile` endpoint has authentication (`@token_required`) commented out (lines 152-153). Any unauthenticated caller can retrieve the full profile of any user—including their email, role, permissions, and team—by passing an arbitrary `user_id` integer as a query parameter.

cwe: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

extra.message: The `GET /api/v1/profile` endpoint accepts a `user_id` query parameter and returns that user's profile data (username, email, country, role, permissions, team) without any authorization check to verify the caller is entitled to view that profile. Even if authentication were re-enabled, any authenticated user could enumerate all other users' sensitive profile data by iterating `user_id` values.

cwe: CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

extra.message: `requests.get(picture_url)` fetches an arbitrary URL supplied by the user in the `picture_url` JSON field with no validation, allowlist, or SSRF protection. An attacker can supply internal network addresses (e.g., `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/`) to probe internal services, cloud metadata endpoints, or other hosts not intended to be reachable from outside.

The uploaded file's original filename (`file.filename`) is used without sanitization when constructing the save path: `filepath = os.path.join(UPLOAD_FOLDER, filename)`. An attacker can submit a filename containing path traversal sequences (e.g., `../../etc/cron.d/backdoor`) to write files outside the intended upload directory. `werkzeug.utils.secure_filename()` is not called.

cwe: CWE-22: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal')

extra.message: Passwords are hashed using MD5 (`hashlib.md5(password.encode()).hexdigest()`) with no salt. MD5 is a fast, cryptographically broken hash; password databases hashed this way are trivially cracked via rainbow tables or GPU-accelerated brute force. A modern slow KDF such as bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2 must be used instead.

cwe: CWE-916: Use of Password Hash With Insufficient Computational Effort

extra.message: The Flask `SECRET_KEY` is hardcoded as the literal string `"just_another_secret"`. This key is used to sign session cookies and JWT tokens. Any attacker who reads this source file (e.g., via a public repository) can forge arbitrary session cookies or craft valid JWTs for any user.

`SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY = False` disables the `HttpOnly` flag on the Flask session cookie. This means client-side JavaScript can access the session cookie, enabling cookie theft via any XSS vulnerability present in the application.

`DEBUG = True` is hardcoded in the configuration class. When Flask runs in debug mode it exposes an interactive Werkzeug debugger (with a PIN) and detailed stack traces to clients on errors. If the PIN is bypassed or the debugger is accessible, it allows remote code execution. Even without debugger access, full stack traces leak internal paths, library versions, and logic.

cwe: CWE-94: Improper Control of Generation of Code ('Code Injection')

extra.message: `return {'message': str(e)}, 500` returns the raw Python exception message directly to the API caller. This can expose internal file paths, library names, network topology, and other sensitive implementation details useful to an attacker for reconnaissance.

cwe: CWE-209: Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information

extra.message: The `POST /update_profile` route reads `role` and `permissions` directly from the form submission (`request.form['role']` and `request.form['permissions']`) and writes them to the database for any authenticated user. Although the HTML form has these fields commented out, the server-side handler unconditionally accepts them from any POST body. An attacker can craft a raw HTTP request including `role=admin&permissions=all` to escalate their privileges.

cwe: CWE-915: Improperly Controlled Modification of Dynamically-Determined Object Attributes

extra.message: State-changing POST endpoints (`/update_profile`, `/upload`, `/delete/<filename>`) do not implement any CSRF protection (no CSRF token in forms, no `SameSite` cookie attribute configured, no `flask-wtf` or equivalent library). An attacker can host a page that causes a logged-in victim's browser to silently submit these forms, modifying profile data, uploading files, or deleting files on behalf of the victim.
